items.title: AMOS Stealer delivered via Cursor AI agent session
items.description: On April 23, 2026, Field Effect MDR identified AMOS Stealer malware delivered through a novel technique exploiting Cursor AI agent sessions running Claude Code. The attack employed social engineering to manipulate operators into prompting the AI agent to download and execute malicious AppleScript loaders. The heavily obfuscated scripts performed sandbox evasion checks, collected sensitive data inc
items.ai_summary: The AMOS Stealer malware is delivered through a novel technique exploiting Cursor AI agent sessions running Claude Code, leveraging social engineering to manipulate operators into prompting the AI agent to download and execute malicious AppleScript loaders. The attack employs heavily obfuscated scripts that perform sandbox evasion checks and collect sensitive data, with the potential to impair defenses and steal or forge authentication certificates. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to significant data breaches and unauthorized access, particularly affecting organizations that utilize Cursor AI agent sessions. To remediate, defenders should immediately update their Cursor AI agent sessions to the latest version, ensure that all AppleScript loaders are thoroughly vetted, and implement robust sandbox evasion detection mechanisms. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to T1059.002, T1204, T1027, T1497, T1562, and T1649, and consider implementing enhanced security controls to prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1562| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cloud Atlas activity in the second half of 2025 and early 2026: new tools and a new payload
items.description: Cloud Atlas APT group targeted government organizations and commercial companies in Russia and Belarus during late 2025 and early 2026, employing phishing campaigns with malicious ZIP archives containing LNK shortcuts. The attackers deployed multiple backdoors including VBCloud for file theft and PowerShower for network reconnaissance. New tools identified include PowerCloud, which exfiltrates dat
items.ai_summary: The Cloud Atlas APT group has been targeting government organizations and commercial companies in Russia and Belarus through phishing campaigns, utilizing malicious ZIP archives containing LNK shortcuts to deploy multiple backdoors, including VBCloud for file theft and PowerShower for network reconnaissance. The attackers have also been using new tools such as PowerCloud to exfiltrate data. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to significant data breaches and network compromise, with real-world impact including the theft of sensitive information and disruption of critical services. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and employee education programs to prevent phishing attacks, and ensure that all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches, specifically focusing on securing remote access software and monitoring for suspicious network activity. Additionally, defenders should prioritize network segmentation and encryption to limit the spread of malware and protect sensitive data.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Tracking Iranian APT Screening Serpens’ 2026 Espionage Campaigns
items.description: Unit 42 researchers identified six new remote access Trojan variants deployed by Iran-nexus APT group Screening Serpens between February and April 2026, coinciding with a regional conflict starting February 28, 2026. The group targeted entities in the U.S., Israel, UAE, and other Middle Eastern locations, primarily focusing on technology sector professionals through highly tailored social engineer
items.ai_summary: The Screening Serpens APT group has been identified as deploying six new remote access Trojan variants between February and April 2026, targeting technology sector professionals in the U.S., Israel, UAE, and other Middle Eastern locations. The group's primary attack vector involves highly tailored social engineering tactics to gain initial access, leveraging T1219 (Remote Access Software) to establish a foothold. If exploited, the malware allows the threat actors to remotely access and control compromised systems, potentially leading to data breaches and espionage. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering and employee education programs to prevent social engineering attacks, and ensure all remote access software is up-to-date and configured with strong authentication mechanisms. Additionally, network traffic monitoring and incident response plans should be in place to quickly detect and respond to potential intrusions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Fast and Furious - Nimbus Manticore Operations During the Iranian Conflict
items.description: The Iranian IRGC-affiliated threat actor Nimbus Manticore launched sophisticated cyber operations during Operation Epic Fury, the US military campaign against Iran beginning February 28, 2026. The campaigns targeted organizations in aviation and software sectors across the United States, Europe, and Middle East using career-themed phishing lures. For the first time, the actor employed SEO poisonin
items.ai_summary: The Nimbus Manticore threat actor, affiliated with the Iranian IRGC, has launched targeted cyber operations against organizations in the aviation and software sectors across the United States, Europe, and Middle East. The campaigns utilize career-themed phishing lures, as well as SEO poisoning for the first time, to gain initial access to targeted systems. If exploited, these attacks can lead to significant data breaches and disruption of critical services, with potential real-world impact including compromised flight safety and intellectual property theft. To remediate these threats, defenders should implement robust email filtering and user education programs, and monitor for suspicious search engine optimization activity. Additionally, defenders should ensure all systems are configured to require user interaction for executable files, and implement application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized code execution.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1608.006| C[Resource Development]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1055| E[Collection]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From edge appliance to enterprise compromise: Multi-stage Linux intrusion via F5 and Confluence
items.description: A sophisticated multi-stage intrusion began with the compromise of an internet-facing F5 BIG-IP load balancer running an end-of-life version. The threat actor established SSH access to a Linux server using privileged credentials, then conducted extensive reconnaissance including network scanning with Nmap and service enumeration with gowitness. Following horizontal and vertical scanning operations
items.ai_summary: The threat actor exploited an end-of-life F5 BIG-IP load balancer to gain initial access, then used SSH to access a Linux server with privileged credentials. The actor conducted reconnaissance using tools like Nmap for network scanning and gowitness for service enumeration, allowing them to map the network and identify potential targets. This multi-stage intrusion highlights the importance of keeping systems up-to-date and using strong credentials. To remediate, defenders should immediately update any end-of-life F5 BIG-IP load balancers to the latest version, change all privileged credentials, and implement a robust network monitoring system to detect similar intrusions. Additionally, defenders should limit SSH access to only necessary personnel and implement a least privilege approach to minimize potential damage.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1595| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1046| C[Resource Development]:::tactic
    C -->|T1213| D[Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Laravel Lang Compromised with RCE Backdoor Across 700+ Versions
items.description: Community-maintained Laravel Lang packages were compromised with remote code execution backdoors affecting over 700 versions across multiple repositories including laravel-lang/lang, laravel-lang/http-statuses, laravel-lang/attributes, and laravel-lang/actions. The attack involved coordinated rapid tag publishing on May 22-23, 2026, suggesting organization-level credential compromise. A malicious
items.ai_summary: A coordinated attack compromised community-maintained Laravel Lang packages with remote code execution backdoors across over 700 versions, affecting multiple repositories including laravel-lang/lang, laravel-lang/http-statuses, laravel-lang/attributes, and laravel-lang/actions. The attack vector involved exploiting public-facing applications (T1190) through rapid tag publishing on May 22-23, 2026, suggesting organization-level credential compromise. If exploited, the backdoor allows threat actors to execute arbitrary code, potentially leading to data breaches, ransomware attacks, or privilege escalation. To remediate, developers should immediately update to the latest version (5.0.0) of the affected packages, and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date. Additionally, it is recommended to review and reset all credentials used for publishing packages to prevent further exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Android Trojan Abuses Commercial Rooting Tool and Steals Private Information
items.description: Rootnik is an Android trojan that exploits vulnerabilities in Android 4.3 and earlier by weaponizing a Chinese commercial rooting tool called Root Assistant. The malicious operation spreads through repackaged legitimate applications distributed globally, affecting users primarily in the United States, Malaysia, Thailand, Lebanon and Taiwan. After installation, Rootnik gains root access using stole
items.ai_summary: The Rootnik Android trojan exploits vulnerabilities in Android 4.3 and earlier versions by utilizing a commercial rooting tool called Root Assistant, allowing it to gain root access and steal private information. The trojan spreads through repackaged legitimate applications, primarily affecting users in the United States, Malaysia, Thailand, Lebanon, and Taiwan. To exploit this vulnerability, attackers use the T1068 tactic for privilege escalation, enabling them to access sensitive data. Defenders can remediate this threat by ensuring their Android devices are updated to versions later than 4.3, avoiding the installation of repackaged applications, and using anti-malware software to detect and remove the Rootnik trojan. Additionally, users should be cautious when granting root access to any application, as this can lead to significant security risks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1068| B[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1212| C[Exploitation of Remote Services]:::tactic
    C -->|T1082| D[System Information Discovery]:::tactic
    D -->|T1539| E[Steal Private Information]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Exploitation of KnowledgeDeliver via ViewState Deserialization Vulnerability
items.description: In late 2025, an unknown threat actor exploited a critical zero-day vulnerability in KnowledgeDeliver, a Learning Management System widely used in Japan. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-5426, allowed unauthenticated remote code execution through ViewState deserialization attacks. The issue stemmed from identical hardcoded ASP.NET machine keys distributed across multiple customer deployments
items.ai_summary: A zero-day vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-5426, was exploited in KnowledgeDeliver, a Learning Management System, allowing unauthenticated remote code execution through ViewState deserialization attacks. The issue was caused by identical hardcoded ASP.NET machine keys distributed across multiple customer deployments. This vulnerability was exploited by an unknown threat actor in late 2025, targeting KnowledgeDeliver systems in Japan. To remediate this issue, KnowledgeDeliver customers should update to version 3.5.2 or later, which includes a patch for the ViewState deserialization vulnerability. Additionally, customers should ensure that ASP.NET machine keys are regenerated and not hardcoded across multiple deployments.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic

    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Token Bingo: Don't Let Your Code be the Winner
items.description: In early April 2026, a large-scale device code phishing campaign targeted organizations across multiple sectors and regions, exploiting OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant. Threat actors leveraged the Kali365 phishing-as-a-service platform, originating primarily from IP address 216.203.20[.]95. The campaign used high-fidelity lures directing victims to Microsoft's legitimate device login flow, wh
items.ai_summary: A large-scale device code phishing campaign exploited OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant, targeting organizations across multiple sectors and regions. Threat actors leveraged the Kali365 phishing-as-a-service platform, originating primarily from IP address 216.203.20[.]95. The campaign used high-fidelity lures directing victims to Microsoft's legitimate device login flow, which, if clicked, would have granted attackers access to the victim's device. To prevent such attacks, organizations should ensure that their employees are aware of the phishing tactics and are trained to identify suspicious emails. Additionally, organizations should implement OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant validation and verification to prevent unauthorized access.

    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: RemotePE: The Lazarus RAT that lives in memory
items.description: A sophisticated memory-only toolset used by a North Korean Lazarus subgroup targeting financial and cryptocurrency organizations consists of three malware families forming a chain. DPAPILoader decrypts and loads RemotePELoader from disk using Windows Data Protection API. RemotePELoader beacons to command-and-control servers and retrieves RemotePE, a fully-fledged remote access trojan executed enti
items.ai_summary: RemotePE is a sophisticated memory-only toolset used by a North Korean Lazarus subgroup to target financial and cryptocurrency organizations. The toolset consists of three malware families forming a chain: DPAPILoader, RemotePELoader, and RemotePE. DPAPILoader decrypts and loads RemotePELoader from disk using Windows Data Protection API, which then beacons to command-and-control servers and retrieves RemotePE, a fully-fledged remote access trojan. If exploited, RemotePE can provide threat actors with unauthorized access to sensitive data and systems. To remediate, organizations should ensure that Windows Data Protection API is properly configured and up-to-date, and implement memory-based detection and response capabilities to identify and mitigate potential threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-45401: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate...
items.description: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate entirely offline. Prior to 0.9.5, the validate_url() function in backend/open_webui/retrieval/web/utils.py only validates the initial URL submitted by the caller. The HTTP clients used downstream (sync requests, async aiohttp, langchain's WebBaseLoader) follow HTTP 3xx redirects by default and do not re-validate the r
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-45401 is a critical vulnerability in Open WebUI, a self-hosted AI platform, prior to version 0.9.5. The validate_url() function in backend/open_webui/retrieval/web/utils.py only validates the initial URL submitted by the caller, allowing attackers to exploit HTTP 3xx redirects and bypass validation. This vulnerability can lead to remote code execution (RCE) and data breaches, affecting users who have not updated to the latest version. To remediate, users must update to version 0.9.5 or later, and ensure that HTTP clients used downstream do not follow HTTP 3xx redirects by default. Specifically, users must modify the sync requests, async aiohttp, and langchain's WebBaseLoader to re-validate the redirect URL.
items.workflow_graph:

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-45400: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate...
items.description: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate entirely offline. Prior to 0.9.5, a parsing difference between the urlparse and requests libraries led to an SSRF bypass vulnerability. This vulnerability is fixed in 0.9.5.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-45400 is a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) bypass vulnerability in Open WebUI, a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform, prior to version 0.9.5. The vulnerability arises from a parsing difference between the urlparse and requests libraries, allowing an attacker to bypass SSRF protections. If exploited, an attacker could potentially access internal systems or data, leading to data breaches or privilege escalation. To remediate, users should update to version 0.9.5 or later, as this version addresses the vulnerability. Additionally, users should ensure that the urlparse and requests libraries are properly configured to prevent similar vulnerabilities in the future.

items.title: CVE-2026-45398: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate...
items.description: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate entirely offline. Prior to 0.9.5, _validate_collection_access() checks the user-memory-* and file-* collection name prefixes but does not check knowledge base collections, which use raw UUIDs as collection names. Any authenticated user who knows a private knowledge base UUID can read its content through the retrieval
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-45398 is a vulnerability in Open WebUI, a self-hosted AI platform, prior to version 0.9.5. The issue arises from the _validate_collection_access() function, which fails to check knowledge base collections, allowing authenticated users to read private knowledge base content by knowing a UUID. This vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker with valid credentials, enabling them to access sensitive information. To remediate, update to version 0.9.5 or later, and ensure that knowledge base collections are properly configured. Specifically, administrators should verify that the knowledge base UUIDs are not publicly accessible and implement additional access controls to prevent unauthorized access.

items.title: CVE-2026-45397: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate...
items.description: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate entirely offline. Prior to 0.9.5, GET /api/v1/retrieval/ returns live RAG pipeline configuration to any unauthenticated HTTP client. No Authorization header, cookie, or API key is required. Every adjacent endpoint on the same router (/embedding, /config) is correctly guarded by get_admin_user making this a targeted om
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-45397 is a vulnerability in Open WebUI, a self-hosted AI platform, prior to version 0.9.5. The issue allows unauthenticated HTTP clients to retrieve live RAG pipeline configuration via the GET /api/v1/retrieval/ endpoint. This targeted attack vector enables threat actors to exploit the platform, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data manipulation. To remediate, users should update to version 0.9.5 or later, ensuring the correct configuration of the get_admin_user guard for adjacent endpoints. Additionally, implementing robust authentication and authorization mechanisms can help prevent similar attacks in the future.

items.title: CVE-2026-45396: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate...
items.description: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate entirely offline. Prior to 0.9.5, the POST /api/v1/evaluations/feedback endpoint in Open WebUI v0.9.2 is vulnerable to mass assignment via FeedbackForm, which uses model_config = ConfigDict(extra='allow'). Due to an insecure dictionary merge order in insert_new_feedback(), an authenticated attacker can inject a user_i
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-45396 is a mass assignment vulnerability in Open WebUI v0.9.2 prior to 0.9.5. An authenticated attacker can inject a user_input field into the POST /api/v1/evaluations/feedback endpoint, allowing for arbitrary configuration changes. This vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker with valid credentials, potentially leading to unauthorized access or data tampering. To remediate, update to Open WebUI v0.9.5 or later, and ensure the model_config extra parameter is set to 'deny' in the FeedbackForm. Additionally, consider implementing input validation and sanitization for user-input fields. This vulnerability has a moderate impact, with a CVSS score of 5.4.

items.title: CVE-2026-45386: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate...
items.description: Open WebUI is a self-hosted artificial intelligence platform designed to operate entirely offline. Prior to 0.9.5, Pin/Unpin is a write operation (modifies the message's is_pinned , pinned_by, pinned_at fields), but in standard channels it only checks read permission, allowing users with read-only access to pin/unpin any message. This vulnerability is fixed in 0.9.5.

items.description: Monday recap. Same mess, new week. A sketchy dev tool got people pwned, old bugs came back from the dead, and security products somehow needed protecting from themselves. A bunch of companies spent the week checking old boxes and forgotten servers they should've patched years ago. Good times. Phishing crews are getting smarter too - less obvious scam junk, more targeted stuff that actually

items.title: Ghost CMS Vulnerability Exploited to Hack Over 700 Websites
items.description: Sites belonging to major universities such as Harvard and Oxford, as well as DuckDuckGo, have been compromised in the attack. The post Ghost CMS Vulnerability Exploited to Hack Over 700 Websites appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-05-24, (Mon, May 25th)
items.description: TeamPCP now operates across three package ecosystems in parallel, it reached GitHub&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s own internal codebase, it trojanized an officially Microsoft-published Python SDK, and it appears to have open-sourced its own framework on GitHub.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: FBI warns of Kali365 phishing service targeting Microsoft 365 accounts
items.description: The FBI is warning about the Kali365 phishing-as-a-service platform (PhaaS) that is used to hijack Microsoft 365 accounts by abusing OAuth device code authentication to steal session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication (MFA). [...]

items.title: Oncology Institute Discloses Data Breach
items.description: The affected third-party vendor has not been named, but one possible candidate is TriZetto. The post Oncology Institute Discloses Data Breach appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ghost CMS CVE-2026-26980 Exploited to Hijack 700+ Sites for ClickFix Attacks
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting a recently disclosed critical security flaw in Ghost CMS to inject malicious JavaScript code with an aim to fuel ClickFix attacks. According to QiAnXin XLab, the activity involves the exploitation of CVE-2026-26980 (CVSS score: 9.4), an SQL injection vulnerability in Ghost's Content API that could allow an unauthenticated attacker to read arbitrary data from the

items.title: The Alert Firehose Finally Meets Its Match
items.description: Ask a cybersecurity pro about Network Detection and Response (NDR) and you might still hear "Noisy," "Too much data." But ask the teams running NDR that includes agentic AI capabilities and you'll hear they're actually using it to catch threats earlier, triage faster, and chase fewer false positives. The old complaint lingers in part because reputations are sticky, and because NDR has evolved

items.title: 266,000 Affected by Data Breach at Radiology Associates of Richmond
items.description: Threat actors stole files containing names and protected health information from the healthcare organization’s systems. The post 266,000 Affected by Data Breach at Radiology Associates of Richmond appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Anthropic: Mythos Detected 23,000 Potential Vulnerabilities Across 1,000 OSS Projects
items.description: Many findings have been confirmed to be critical or high-severity vulnerabilities and the number will continue to increase. The post Anthropic: Mythos Detected 23,000 Potential Vulnerabilities Across 1,000 OSS Projects appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Laravel-Lang Packages Poisoned for Malware Delivery
items.description: Published within a 15-minute window, the malicious tags introduced backdoors to exfiltrate CI secrets. The post Laravel-Lang Packages Poisoned for Malware Delivery appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: DocketWise Data Breach Impacts 143,000
items.description: Hackers accessed names, addresses, Social Security numbers, financial information, and medical data from third-party partner repositories. The post DocketWise Data Breach Impacts 143,000 appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Lazarus Deploys RemotePE Memory-Only RAT Against Financial and Crypto Firms
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have shed light on a cross-platform malware called RemotePE that has been put to use by the North Korea-linked Lazarus Group in attacks targeting financial and cryptocurrency organizations. RemotePE, per NCC Group subsidiary Fox-IT, is part of a multi-stage attack chain that involves two loaders tracked as DPAPILoader and RemotePELoader. "DPAPILoader decrypts and

items.title: Over 5,500 GitHub Repositories Infected in ‘Megalodon’ Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Fake automated commits injected GitHub Actions workflows containing payloads to steal credentials, CI secrets, keys, and tokens. The post Over 5,500 GitHub Repositories Infected in ‘Megalodon’ Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: TrapDoor Supply Chain Attack Spreads Credential-Stealing Malware via npm, PyPI, and CratesIO
items.description: A new coordinated cross-ecosystem software supply chain attack campaign has targeted npm, PyPI, and Crates.io to distribute credential-stealing malware. The campaign, codenamed TrapDoor, spans more than 34 malicious packages across over 384 versions. The earliest activity was recorded on May 22, 2026, at 8:20 p.m. UTC, with new packages published to the ecosystems in waves from a cluster of

items.title: Wireshark 4.6.6 Released, (Sun, May 24th)
items.description: Wireshark release 4.6.6 fixes 1 vulnerability and 11 bugs.

items.title: Ghost CMS SQL injection flaw exploited in large-scale ClickFix campaign
items.description: A large-scale campaign is exploiting a critical SQL injection vulnerability (CVE-2026-26980) in Ghost CMS to inject malicious JavaScript code that triggers ClickFix attack flows. [...]

items.title: Laravel Lang packages hijacked to deploy credential-stealing malware
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting the Laravel Lang localization packages has exposed developers to a sophisticated credential-stealing malware campaign after attackers abused GitHub version tags to distribute malicious code through Composer packages. [...]

items.title: npm Adds 2FA-Gated Publishing and Package Install Controls Against Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: GitHub has rolled out new controls for npm to improve the security of the software supply chain, giving maintainers the ability to explicitly approve a release prior to the packages becoming publicly available for installation. Called staged publishing, the feature is now generally available on npm. It mandates that a human maintainer pass a two-factor authentication (2FA) challenge to approve

items.title: Packagist Supply Chain Attack Infects 8 Packages Using GitHub-Hosted Linux Malware
items.description: A new "coordinated" supply chain attack campaign has impacted eight packages on Packagist including malicious code designed to run a Linux binary retrieved from a GitHub Releases URL. "Although the affected packages were all Composer packages, the malicious code was not added to composer.json," Socket said. "Instead, it was inserted into package.json, targeting projects that ship JavaScript

items.title: Italy disrupts CINEMAGOAL piracy app that stole streaming auth codes
items.description: Italian authorities have dismantled a piracy ecosystem centered around the CINEMAGOAL app that provided access to various streaming platforms, including Netflix, Disney+, and Spotify. [...]

items.title: Claude Mythos AI Finds 10,000 High-Severity Flaws in Widely Used Software
items.description: Anthropic on Friday disclosed that Project Glasswing has helped uncover more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across some of the most "systemically" important software across the world since the cybersecurity initiative went live last month. Project Glasswing is a defensive effort launched by the artificial intelligence (AI) company to secure critical global software

items.title: ‘Underminr’ Vulnerability Lets Attackers Hide Malicious Connections Behind Trusted Domains
items.description: The stealthy vulnerability impacts roughly 88 million domains and can be exploited to bypass DNS filtering and hide command-and-control traffic. The post ‘Underminr’ Vulnerability Lets Attackers Hide Malicious Connections Behind Trusted Domains appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Laravel-Lang PHP Packages Compromised to Deliver Cross-Platform Credential Stealer
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a fresh software supply chain attack campaign that has targeted multiple PHP packages belonging to Laravel-Lang to deliver a comprehensive credential-stealing framework. The affected packages include - laravel-lang/lang laravel-lang/http-statuses laravel-lang/attributes laravel-lang/actions "The timing and pattern of the newly published tags

items.title: LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin CVE-2026-48172 Exploited to Run Scripts as Root
items.description: A maximum-severity security vulnerability impacting LiteSpeed User-End cPanel Plugin has come under active exploitation in the wild. The flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-48172 (CVSS score: 10.0), relates to an instance of incorrect privilege assignment that an attacker could abuse to run arbitrary scripts with elevated permissions. "Any cPanel user (including an attacker or a compromised account) may

items.title: An Example of Stack String in High Level Language, (Sat, May 23rd)
items.description: This week, I&#x27m attending the SEC670[1] training (â€œRed Teaming Tools - Developing Windows Implants, Shellcode, Command and Controlâ€). From my point of view, this training fits perfectly with FOR610 or FOR710 (malware analysis) because it addresses malware from the opposite: Instead of performing reverse engineering, you write malicious code! Always interesting to have another point of view.

items.title: CISA to allow researchers to report vulnerabilities to exploited bugs catalog
items.description: The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) announced the creation of a nomination form on Thursday that they said enables “researchers, vendors, and industry partners” to report bugs that need to be added to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog.

items.title: FBI warns of Kali365 phishing-as-a-service after April Microsoft 365 attacks
items.description: The law enforcement agency published an advisory on Thursday about Kali365 — a Telegram-based service for cybercriminals that allows them to capture legitimate "OAuth" tokens enabling widespread access to Microsoft 365 environments.

items.title: Meta settles school district lawsuit claiming addictive design harmed students' mental health
items.description: The bellwether lawsuit was the first of at least 1,200 to be brought by a school district against Meta, Snap, YouTube and TikTok for similar alleged harms. The other cases have not yet been tried.

items.title: Netherlands seizes 800 servers of hosting firm enabling cyberattacks
items.description: Financial crime investigators in the Netherlands (FIOD) arrested two men and seized 800 servers linked to a web hosting company that enabled cyberattacks, interference operations, and disinformation campaigns. [...]

items.title: Drupal Vulnerability in Hacker Crosshairs Shortly After Disclosure
items.description: Drupal is warning users that it has already seen attempts to exploit CVE-2026-9082 and security firms are seeing attacks against thousands of websites. The post Drupal Vulnerability in Hacker Crosshairs Shortly After Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Akamai Joins Growing Chorus of Vendors Betting Big on Secure Enterprise Browsers
items.description: When Akamai announced its LayerX acquisition, the company joined a growing list of vendors adding secure enterprise browsers to their product portfolios.

items.title: Former US execs plead guilty to aiding tech support scammers
items.description: Two former executives of a call-tracking and analytics company pleaded guilty to concealing a years-long tech support fraud scheme that victimized individuals worldwide. [...]

items.title: Why the Supreme Court's Chatrie case could change the meaning of privacy in America
items.description: Lawyer Adam Unikowsky spoke with Recorded Future News about why he believes geofence searches are problematic and why the way the court rules could have a dramatic impact on Americans’ right to privacy.

items.title: Canadian man arrested, charged for running KimWolf DDos botnet
items.description: In court documents unsealed on Thursday, the Justice Department said Jacob Butler ran KimWolf as a DDoS-for-hire service that infected over a million devices worldwide.

items.title: In Other News: Industrial Router Exploitation, CISA KEV Nomination Form, Gas Station Hacking
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: CISA contractor exposes credentials, Mythos testing and new features, Huawei router flaw triggered telecom blackout. The post In Other News: Industrial Router Exploitation, CISA KEV Nomination Form, Gas Station Hacking appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Trend Micro warns of Apex One zero-day exploited in the wild
items.description: Japanese cybersecurity software company Trend Micro has addressed an Apex One zero-day vulnerability exploited in attacks targeting Windows systems. [...]

items.title: Verizon DBIR: Healthcare Fends Off Increased Social Engineering Attacks
items.description: Ransomware and vendor breaches persist. The "2026 Data Breach Investigations Report" (DBIR) highlights how evolving social engineering tactics make the sector more vulnerable.

items.title: Drupal: Critical SQL injection flaw now targeted in attacks
items.description: Drupal is warning that hackers are attempting to exploit a "highly critical" SQL injection vulnerability announced earlier this week. [...]

items.title: Why Chargebacks are Just One Piece of the Fraud Puzzle
items.description: Fraud losses don't stop at chargebacks. False declines, account takeovers, and abuse also damage revenue and trust. IPQS breaks down why fraud teams need broader visibility into risk and customer impact. [...]

items.title: China's Webworm Uses Discord, Microsoft Graphs to Hack EU Governments
items.description: The advanced persistent threat group also relied on SOCKS proxies like SoftEther VPN, tunneling tools that act as a middleman between victim and attacker.

items.title: Cross-Platform NPM Stealer, (Fri, May 22nd)
items.description: I found a Node.js stealer that looked pretty well obfuscated. The file was not running out-of-the-box because it was uploaded on VT as â€œextracted-decoded.jsâ€ (and reformated). The SHA256 is 049300aa5dd774d6c984779a0570f59610399c71864b5d5c2605906db46ddeb9[1]. It did not run properly in a sandbox so only a static analysis was performed.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, May 22nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9942, (Fri, May 22nd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: Google API Keys Remain Active After Deletion
items.description: A security researcher discovered the API keys can still be used for up to 23 minutes after deletion, even though the cloud provider claims deletion is immediate.

items.title: AI Agents Are Shifting Identity Security Budget Dynamics
items.description: AI agent projects are proliferating throughout the enterprise, and those AI agent identities require management, security, and governance. New Omdia research shows the AI agent identity budget dynamics are very different than traditional IAM projects.

items.title: Chinese APTs Share Linux Backdoor in Central Asia Telco Attacks
items.description: "Showboat" doesn't show off, but clearly it doesn't need to, as it's long helped China spy on small market communications providers.

items.title: Selective HTTP Proxying in Linux, (Thu, May 21st)
items.description: Recently, Rob wrote about a tool, Proxifier, that can intercept requests from specific processes. Proxifier is available for Windows, macOS, and Android. But I have not seen a generic Linux option yet. The advantage of a tool like Proxifier is the ability to target specific software. For debugging, reverse engineering, and similar tasks, selecting a specific process is quite useful, as it creates less noise to sift through and simplifies analysis.

items.title: Content Delivery Exploit Opens Websites to Brand Hijacking
items.description: The Underminr domain-fronting attack allows threat actors to modify Web requests and leverage trusted websites to cloak malicious activity.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
ISC Stormcast For Thursday, May 21st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9940, (Thu, May 21st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Cyber Pros Can't Decide If AI Is a Good or a Bad Thing
items.description: There is nothing cybersecurity professionals are more excited about, and nothing they fear more, than AI.

items.title: GitHub Confirms Breach, 4K Internal Repos Stolen
items.description: GitHub confirmed a data breach this week involving the theft of thousands of developer code repositories. One threat actor — TeamPCP — took credit.

items.title: Fake Android Apps Commit Carrier Billing Fraud for Premium Services
items.description: The disguised apps use WebView automation, JavaScript injection, and OTP interception to avoid detection and complete fraudulent subscriptions.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, May 20th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9938, (Wed, May 20th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
